title: Collective Redundancies and Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) (Amendment) Regulations 2014
jurisdiction: England, Wales, and Scotland (not Northern Ireland)
chapters: 11 regulations. Amends BOTH TULRCA 1992 (collective redundancy consultation) AND TUPE 2006 (transfer of undertakings). Six key amendments: (1) pre-transfer collective redundancy consultation by the transferee, (2) service provision change clarification, (3) greater flexibility to vary terms post-transfer, (4) static approach to collective agreements, (5) employee liability information deadline extended to 28 days, (6) micro-business direct consultation. CRITICAL INTERPLAY NOTE: This instrument sits at the intersection of THREE major statutory frameworks — collective redundancy (TULRCA s.188), TUPE (SI 2006/246), and unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.98). The ERA 2025 introduces further changes to collective redundancy consultation (2027) that will affect how this instrument operates.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Statutory Instrument — made under TULRCA 1992 s.288, ERA 1996 s.236, and European Communities Act 1972 s.2(2)
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills (Coalition Government), following consultation and a call for evidence on TUPE reform
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Three commencement dates reflecting different lead-in requirements: main amendments (31 January 2014), employee liability information extended deadline (1 May 2014), micro-business direct consultation (31 July 2014).

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — collective redundancy consultation reform
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2025 introduces changes to collective redundancy consultation expected in 2027: (1) The 'one establishment' threshold will change — employers will need to consider the total number of redundancies across the WHOLE ORGANISATION, not just individual workplaces. This fundamentally changes when collective consultation is triggered. (2) The maximum protective award for failure to collectively consult is DOUBLED from 90 to 180 days' pay (from April 2026). These changes will significantly affect how the pre-transfer consultation provisions of this instrument operate in practice.

precedingDocuments.title: TUPE 2006 (SI 2006/246) — the parent Regulations
precedingDocuments.significance: The Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006 implement the EU Acquired Rights Directive. They protect employees when a business is transferred. The 2014 Regulations AMEND TUPE 2006 — they do not replace it.

precedingDocuments.title: TULRCA 1992 ss.188-198 — collective redundancy consultation
precedingDocuments.significance: TULRCA requires collective consultation when 20+ employees are proposed for redundancy at one establishment within 90 days. The 2014 Regulations inserted s.198A allowing pre-transfer consultation by the transferee.

leadingCases.name: USDAW v WW Realisation 1 Ltd (Woolworths)
leadingCases.principle: The 'one establishment' test for collective redundancy consultation means the INDIVIDUAL workplace where the employees work, not the employer's entire organisation. So an employer proposing 15 redundancies at Site A and 15 at Site B does NOT trigger the 20+ threshold at either site — even though 30 redundancies are proposed in total.
leadingCases.heldText: The word 'establishment' in TULRCA s.188 means the local unit or entity where the affected employees are assigned to carry out their duties.
leadingCases.significance: This interpretation is being CHANGED by the ERA 2025 (expected 2027). The ERA 2025 will require employers to consider the total number of redundancies across the WHOLE ORGANISATION, not just individual establishments. This will trigger collective consultation in many more situations than the current 'one establishment' test.

parts.title: Part 1 — The Six Key Amendments and Their Practical Impact

sections.title: What changed, why it changed, and what it means for employers managing transfers and redundancies
sections.summary: The 2014 Regulations made six targeted amendments to the TUPE 2006 and TULRCA 1992 frameworks. Each amendment responded to practical difficulties identified through a government call for evidence and consultation. The amendments give employers more FLEXIBILITY in managing transfers and post-transfer restructuring, while maintaining the core protections for transferring employees.
sections.text:
THE SIX KEY AMENDMENTS:

═══ AMENDMENT 1 — PRE-TRANSFER COLLECTIVE REDUNDANCY CONSULTATION (Reg.3, inserting TULRCA s.198A) ═══

THE PROBLEM: Before 2014, if a transferee (the acquiring employer) planned post-transfer redundancies, it could only begin collective consultation AFTER the transfer completed. This caused delays — the transferee had to wait until the transfer date, then begin the 30/45 day consultation period, before any redundancies could take effect.

THE SOLUTION: The transferee can now ELECT to begin collective redundancy consultation BEFORE the transfer, provided:
(a) There will be (or is likely to be) a relevant transfer.
(b) The transferee proposes to dismiss 20+ employees at one establishment within 90 days.
(c) The transferor consents in writing.
(d) The transferor is not OBLIGED to provide information or assistance (but may do so voluntarily).

PRACTICAL IMPACT: This enables the transferee to begin restructuring planning EARLIER, providing greater certainty for affected employees sooner. Pre-transfer consultation counts towards the 30/45 day minimum consultation period.

CLIENT SCENARIO: A company acquires a business and plans to merge it with existing operations, resulting in 50 redundancies. Previously, it had to wait until the transfer completed before beginning the 45-day consultation. Now, with the transferor's consent, it can begin consultation before the transfer — potentially starting redundancies weeks earlier after transfer.

═══ AMENDMENT 2 — SERVICE PROVISION CHANGE CLARIFICATION (Reg.5, amending TUPE Reg.3) ═══

THE PROBLEM: The 'service provision change' concept (outsourcing, insourcing, re-tendering of contracts) was the subject of considerable uncertainty and litigation.

THE SOLUTION: Added a new Reg.3(2A) to TUPE 2006 clarifying that a service provision change requires an 'organised grouping of employees' whose principal purpose is carrying out the relevant activities on behalf of the client. This reflected existing case law (Churchill Dulwich v Metropolitan Resources [2009]) but provided statutory certainty.

PRACTICAL IMPACT: Confirms that TUPE applies to outsourcing, insourcing, and contract re-tendering where there is an identifiable group of employees dedicated to the relevant service.

═══ AMENDMENT 3 — GREATER FLEXIBILITY TO VARY TERMS POST-TRANSFER (Reg.6, amending TUPE Reg.4) ═══

THE PROBLEM: Under TUPE, variations to employment terms are VOID if the sole or principal reason is the transfer itself. This made it extremely difficult for transferees to harmonise terms with their existing workforce — even where the changes were beneficial or neutral.

THE SOLUTION: The 2014 Regulations provide that the restriction on varying contracts does NOT apply where:
(a) The contract ITSELF permits the variation (e.g., a mobility clause allowing relocation), OR
(b) The variation is for an ETO (economic, technical, or organisational) reason entailing 'changes in the workforce' — which NOW explicitly includes changes to the WORKPLACE (location).

Additionally, 'changes in the workforce' was explicitly expanded to include changes to the place of work, not just changes to the number or functions of employees.

PRACTICAL IMPACT: Transferees have more scope to restructure post-transfer. Relocating a transferred workforce to a new location can now be an ETO reason. BUT: if the employee is dismissed as a result and the reason is the transfer, the dismissal is still automatically unfair unless an ETO reason applies.

═══ AMENDMENT 4 — STATIC APPROACH TO COLLECTIVE AGREEMENTS (Reg.6, inserting TUPE Reg.4A) ═══

THE PROBLEM: Under TUPE, collective agreement terms that were incorporated into individual contracts transferred with the employee. But it was unclear whether FUTURE changes to the collective agreement (negotiated after the transfer) also bound the transferee — who might not be party to the collective bargaining.

THE SOLUTION: New Reg.4A provides that where a contract incorporates provisions of collective agreements 'as may be agreed from time to time', the transferee is NOT bound by changes to the collective agreement made AFTER the transfer, provided:
(a) The provision is agreed AFTER the transfer date, AND
(b) The transferee is NOT a participant in the collective bargaining.

Additionally, terms derived from collective agreements may be renegotiated after ONE YEAR post-transfer, provided the overall package is no less favourable.

PRACTICAL IMPACT: The transferee is not permanently locked into dynamic collective agreements it did not negotiate. This was a significant concern for private sector acquirers of public sector services, where terms were set by national collective bargaining.

═══ AMENDMENT 5 — EMPLOYEE LIABILITY INFORMATION DEADLINE EXTENDED (Reg.10, amending TUPE Reg.11) ═══

THE CHANGE: The deadline for the transferor to provide employee liability information to the transferee was extended from 14 days to 28 DAYS before the transfer.

Employee liability information includes: identity and age of each employee, ERA s.1 written statement particulars, disciplinary and grievance records from the last 2 years, applicable collective agreements, and any legal proceedings (actual or reasonably anticipated).

PRACTICAL IMPACT: The transferee has more time to process information about transferring employees, conduct due diligence, and plan for post-transfer integration. Essential for payroll setup, benefit enrolment, and restructuring planning.

═══ AMENDMENT 6 — MICRO-BUSINESS DIRECT CONSULTATION (Reg.10, inserting TUPE Reg.13A) ═══

THE CHANGE: Employers with FEWER THAN 10 EMPLOYEES may directly consult affected employees about a transfer, rather than going through elected representatives, where:
(a) There is no recognised trade union, AND
(b) There are no existing appropriate representatives, AND
(c) The employer has not invited employees to elect representatives.

PRACTICAL IMPACT: Reduces the administrative burden on micro-businesses. For a 6-person business being acquired, the employer can speak directly to the affected employees rather than going through a formal election of representatives.
sections.keyPrinciple: The 2014 Regulations did not revolutionise TUPE or collective redundancy law — they made targeted, practical amendments to address real-world difficulties. The core TUPE protections remain: employees transfer automatically, on their existing terms, and dismissal because of the transfer is automatically unfair unless an ETO reason applies. What changed is the FLEXIBILITY available to employers in managing the practical consequences of a transfer — earlier consultation, clearer rules on varying terms, and a static approach to collective agreements.
refs: ERA 1996 s.98 — unfair dismissal (ETO reason = potentially fair)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment Rights on the Transfer of an Undertaking (TUPE) — BIS Guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on TUPE 2006 as amended by the 2014 Regulations, including worked examples and practical scenarios.

commonErrors: Not knowing that pre-transfer collective consultation is possible — since 2014, the transferee can begin collective redundancy consultation BEFORE the transfer, with the transferor's written consent. This can save weeks in the restructuring timeline

Assuming that ALL post-transfer term variations are void — the 2014 amendments allow variations where the contract permits it OR where there is an ETO reason. 'Changes in the workforce' now includes changes to the WORKPLACE (location). But variations purely because of the transfer remain void

Believing the transferee is bound by future collective agreement changes — under Reg.4A, the transferee is NOT bound by collective agreement changes made after the transfer if the transferee is not a party to the bargaining. This is the 'static' approach

Not providing employee liability information at least 28 days before transfer — the deadline was extended from 14 to 28 days. The transferor who provides information late (or not at all) faces a tribunal complaint and compensation of at least £500 per employee

Not understanding the interaction between TUPE dismissals and collective redundancy — if a transferee proposes 20+ post-transfer redundancies at one establishment within 90 days, collective consultation is required REGARDLESS of whether the redundancies are connected to the transfer. The obligation is SEPARATE from the TUPE information and consultation obligations

Forgetting that the 'one establishment' test is about to CHANGE — the ERA 2025 (expected 2027) will require employers to count redundancies across the WHOLE ORGANISATION, not just individual workplaces. This will trigger collective consultation in many more transfer-related redundancy situations

versions.text: IN FORCE: all six amendments operational. Pre-transfer consultation (s.198A), service provision change clarification, greater term variation flexibility, static collective agreements, 28-day employee liability information deadline, micro-business direct consultation.
versions.amendedBy: The Collective Redundancies and Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) (Amendment) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/16)
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government sought to increase employer flexibility in managing transfers while maintaining core employee protections. The amendments responded to practical difficulties identified through a call for evidence and consultation.

parts.title: Part 2 — The Interplay Map: How This Instrument Connects to the Wider Framework

sections.title: The legislative ecosystem for transfers and collective redundancy — and what the ERA 2025 changes
sections.summary: This instrument cannot be understood in isolation. It amends two parent instruments (TUPE 2006 and TULRCA 1992) and interacts with unfair dismissal law (ERA 1996) and the ERA 2025 reforms. Eileen must understand all four dimensions to advise on any transfer or collective redundancy situation.
sections.text:
THE INTERPLAY MAP:

═══ TUPE 2006 — WHAT TRANSFERS ═══
When a business (or part of a business) is transferred, or a service provision changes:
(a) EMPLOYEES transfer automatically to the new employer — on their EXISTING terms and conditions.
(b) The new employer inherits ALL rights, duties, and liabilities in relation to those employees.
(c) Dismissal BECAUSE OF the transfer is automatically unfair — UNLESS there is an ETO reason.
(d) The transferor and transferee must INFORM AND CONSULT affected employees (TUPE Regs 13-16).
(e) The transferor must provide EMPLOYEE LIABILITY INFORMATION at least 28 days before transfer (2014 amendment).

═══ TULRCA 1992 s.188 — WHEN COLLECTIVE CONSULTATION IS REQUIRED ═══
When an employer proposes to dismiss 20+ employees at one establishment within 90 days:
(a) 20-99 proposed redundancies: at least 30 DAYS consultation before any dismissals.
(b) 100+ proposed redundancies: at least 45 DAYS consultation.
(c) Consultation must be with appropriate representatives (trade union if recognised, otherwise elected representatives).
(d) Must cover: ways of avoiding dismissals, reducing numbers, mitigating consequences.
(e) Must notify the Secretary of State (HR1 form).
(f) Failure: PROTECTIVE AWARD of up to 90 days' pay per employee (DOUBLED to 180 days from April 2026 under ERA 2025).

═══ ERA 1996 s.98 — THE FAIRNESS TEST ═══
Where the dismissal is for an ETO reason entailing changes in the workforce:
(a) The dismissal is NOT automatically unfair (the TUPE protection is disapplied).
(b) The dismissal is potentially fair as redundancy (ERA s.98(2)(c)) or 'some other substantial reason' (ERA s.98(1)(b)).
(c) BUT: the employer must still follow a FAIR PROCEDURE (Polkey). The ETO reason makes the dismissal potentially fair — it does not excuse procedural unfairness.

═══ ERA 2025 — WHAT'S CHANGING ═══

(1) PROTECTIVE AWARD DOUBLED — April 2026:
The maximum protective award for failure to collectively consult increases from 90 to 180 days' pay per affected employee. For a transfer involving 50+ redundancies, the financial exposure for failure to consult is now enormous.

(2) 'ONE ESTABLISHMENT' TEST CHANGING — 2027:
The USDAW v Woolworths 'one establishment' interpretation will be REVERSED. Employers will need to count redundancies across the WHOLE ORGANISATION, not just individual sites. This means:
— A retailer closing 5 shops, each with 10 employees, who currently does NOT trigger collective consultation (each site is under 20) WILL trigger it under the ERA 2025 (50 redundancies across the organisation).
— In a TUPE context: a transferee with multiple sites who makes smaller numbers of redundancies at each will now need to count the total.

(3) UNFAIR DISMISSAL QUALIFYING PERIOD REDUCED — January 2027:
The qualifying period drops from 2 years to 6 months. Combined with the removal of the compensatory award cap, this increases the risk of unfair dismissal claims from transferred employees who are subsequently dismissed.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR EMPLOYERS MANAGING TRANSFERS:

(a) DUE DILIGENCE is critical — request employee liability information early (28-day minimum). Understand the transferring workforce: numbers, terms, collective agreements, pending claims.
(b) PLAN RESTRUCTURING before completion — use the 2014 pre-transfer consultation provisions where possible.
(c) DON'T ASSUME you can change terms post-transfer — variations are void unless contractually permitted or for a genuine ETO reason.
(d) DON'T ASSUME collective agreements continue to bind you dynamically — the static approach (Reg.4A) means you are not bound by future changes you didn't negotiate.
(e) COUNT REDUNDANCIES across the organisation from 2027 — the 'one establishment' test is changing.
(f) BUDGET for the doubled protective award from April 2026 — failure to collectively consult is now twice as expensive.
sections.keyPrinciple: This instrument sits at a junction where three areas of law meet. The employer managing a transfer must simultaneously understand TUPE (automatic transfer of employees), collective redundancy (consultation obligations), and unfair dismissal (the ETO defence). The 2014 Regulations eased some practical difficulties but the core framework remains protective of employees. The ERA 2025 changes (doubled protective award from April 2026, organisation-wide counting from 2027) will make compliance MORE demanding, not less.
refs: ERA 2025 — collective redundancy reforms (2027), protective award doubled (April 2026)
USDAW v WW Realisation 1 Ltd (Woolworths) [2015] EWCA Civ 1264
commonErrors: Treating TUPE information and consultation obligations as the SAME as collective redundancy consultation — they are SEPARATE obligations with different triggers, different requirements, and different remedies. An employer can comply with one and breach the other

Not preparing for the 'one establishment' test change in 2027 — employers who currently avoid collective consultation by counting redundancies at individual sites will need to count across the whole organisation. This is a fundamental change that requires advance planning

Not budgeting for the doubled protective award from April 2026 — the maximum increases from 90 to 180 days' pay per affected employee. For 50 employees on average earnings, the maximum exposure increases from approximately £675,000 to £1,350,000

Dismissing transferred employees in the first few months and assuming the 2-year qualifying period protects against unfair dismissal claims — from January 2027, the qualifying period reduces to 6 months. Transferred employees retain their continuous service and may already have the qualifying period. Even without it, TUPE-related dismissals are AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR regardless of service length

parts.title: Part 3 — The HR Advisor's Practical Guide: Process, Edge Cases, and Evolving Obligations

sections.title: Due diligence, employee objections, insolvency, harmonisation, and the complete acquisition workflow
sections.summary: This section transforms the legal framework into practical HR guidance. It addresses the scenarios that experienced HR professionals encounter most frequently — and the edge cases that catch them out. It also documents how Eileen's responses must EVOLVE as the ERA 2025 provisions take effect in phases.
sections.text:
TUPE DUE DILIGENCE CHECKLIST — WHAT TO REQUEST AND VERIFY BEFORE A TRANSFER:

(1) EMPLOYEE LIABILITY INFORMATION (must be provided 28 days before transfer):
□ Full list of transferring employees — names, ages, start dates, continuous service dates
□ Section 1 written statement for each employee (or contract of employment)
□ Details of any disciplinary action taken in the last 2 years
□ Details of any grievances raised in the last 2 years
□ Details of any legal proceedings (actual or reasonably anticipated) by or against any transferring employee
□ Details of any collective agreements that apply

(2) ADDITIONAL DUE DILIGENCE (not required by TUPE but essential for planning):
□ Organisational chart of transferring workforce
□ Pay and benefits data — base salary, bonuses, commission, pension, car allowance, private healthcare, share schemes
□ Working patterns — hours, shifts, flexible working arrangements, homeworking
□ Absence data — sickness absence levels, anyone on long-term sick leave, maternity/paternity/SPL
□ Trade union recognition agreements
□ Pending or anticipated tribunal claims, settlement agreements, or ongoing disputes
□ Restrictive covenants and confidentiality agreements
□ Agency workers, contractors, and consultants engaged by the transferor
□ TUPE history — has the workforce transferred before? Are there inherited liabilities from previous transfers?

(3) RED FLAGS in due diligence:
⚠️ Transferor reluctant to provide information — may indicate hidden liabilities
⚠️ High number of pending grievances or tribunal claims
⚠️ Significant gap between transferor's terms and your existing terms — harmonisation will be difficult
⚠️ Complex pension arrangements — especially defined benefit schemes
⚠️ Trade union recognition with collective agreements — you may inherit obligations

EMPLOYEE OBJECTION TO TRANSFER (TUPE Reg.4(7)-4(9)):

This is a critical scenario Eileen MUST handle:

An employee has the RIGHT to OBJECT to transferring. If they do:
(a) The employee's contract of employment TERMINATES on the date of the transfer.
(b) This is NOT a dismissal — the employee CANNOT claim unfair dismissal.
(c) The employee has NO entitlement to redundancy pay (because they have not been dismissed).
(d) The employee has NO entitlement to notice pay (unless constructive dismissal applies — see below).
(e) Continuous service is BROKEN.

EXCEPTION — Constructive Dismissal (TUPE Reg.4(9)):
If the transfer involves (or would involve) a SUBSTANTIAL CHANGE in working conditions to the material detriment of the employee, the employee may treat the contract as terminated by the employer. In this case:
(a) The termination IS treated as a dismissal.
(b) The employee CAN claim unfair dismissal (and the TUPE automatic unfairness provisions apply).
(c) The employee MAY be entitled to redundancy pay.

Examples of substantial detrimental change: significant relocation, major change in role or responsibilities, significant reduction in status, removal of a core benefit.

Practical advice for Eileen to give employees:
— Think very carefully before objecting. You lose your job, your continuous service, your redundancy rights, and your right to claim unfair dismissal.
— If the transfer involves a substantial detrimental change, you may have BETTER legal remedies by transferring and then claiming constructive dismissal (preserving your continuous service and redundancy rights).
— Seek legal advice before objecting.

INSOLVENCY + TUPE (TUPE Reg.8):

When the transferor is subject to RELEVANT INSOLVENCY PROCEEDINGS (proceedings aimed at rescuing the business, not terminal winding-up):

(a) Employees STILL transfer to the transferee.
(b) BUT: certain debts (statutory redundancy pay, arrears of pay up to 8 weeks, notice pay up to 6 weeks, holiday pay up to 6 weeks, unpaid pension contributions) do NOT transfer. They are paid from the NATIONAL INSURANCE FUND (ERA 1996 ss.166-170).
(c) The transferee and representatives can agree to vary terms WITHOUT an ETO reason — provided the agreement is reached with appropriate representatives and is designed to safeguard employment opportunities.
(d) Where the insolvency is TERMINAL (liquidation, winding-up), TUPE may not apply at all — the employees are dismissed by the insolvent employer and claim from the National Insurance Fund.

This distinction matters enormously. An HR manager advising on a rescue acquisition from administration needs to understand which debts transfer and which are covered by the Fund.

DOWNWARD HARMONISATION — THE PRACTICAL TRAP:

Scenario: You acquire a business. The transferred employees earn £35,000. Your existing staff doing the same work earn £30,000. You want to harmonise at £30,000.

The law:
(a) You CANNOT reduce pay if the sole or principal reason is the transfer — the variation is VOID.
(b) Even if you have an ETO reason (e.g., reducing costs to ensure business survival), reducing pay may trigger constructive dismissal claims if it is a fundamental breach of contract.
(c) You CAN harmonise UPWARD — bringing your existing staff up to the transferred employees' level. This costs more but avoids legal risk.
(d) You CAN wait. After 12+ months, if the reason for the variation is genuinely separate from the transfer (e.g., a company-wide restructure), the transfer is less likely to be the sole or principal reason.
(e) The practical approach: use s.111A protected conversations to explore whether transferred employees would accept a revised package in exchange for other benefits (bonus, flexible working, enhanced pension).

Risk: If you unilaterally reduce pay, the employee can:
— Claim the variation is void under TUPE (and continue to claim the original rate)
— Resign and claim constructive dismissal (automatically unfair if the reason is the transfer)
— Claim unlawful deduction from wages (ERA 1996 s.13)

THE COMPLETE ACQUISITION + REDUNDANCY WORKFLOW:

For an HR manager managing an acquisition with planned redundancies, the integrated timeline:

WEEKS -8 to -4 (before transfer):
□ Request employee liability information (28-day deadline)
□ Conduct due diligence (checklist above)
□ Identify planned redundancies — numbers, roles, locations
□ Assess whether collective consultation is triggered (20+ at one establishment — or from 2027, 20+ across the organisation)
□ If collective consultation required: consider pre-transfer consultation (s.198A — request transferor's written consent)
□ Notify Secretary of State (HR1 form) if 20+ redundancies proposed
□ Begin TUPE information and consultation with affected employees (TUPE Regs 13-16) — this is SEPARATE from collective redundancy consultation

WEEK -2 to TRANSFER DATE:
□ Finalise transfer arrangements
□ Communicate with all transferring employees — their terms transfer, their jobs are protected, the process going forward
□ Set up payroll, benefits, pension for transferring employees
□ Handle any employee objections (rare but must be managed)

TRANSFER DATE:
□ Employment transfers automatically — no action required from employees
□ All rights, duties, and liabilities transfer to the transferee
□ Collective agreements transfer (subject to the static approach for future changes)

POST-TRANSFER (weeks 1-12):
□ Continue/complete collective redundancy consultation (30 or 45 days minimum)
□ Conduct individual redundancy consultations (fair selection, Polkey, suitable alternative employment)
□ Consider s.111A protected conversations for voluntary departures
□ Implement redundancies only AFTER consultation is complete
□ Ensure ETO reason is documented for any TUPE-related dismissals

HOW EILEEN'S RESPONSES MUST EVOLVE:

This instrument is TEMPORALLY DYNAMIC. Eileen must adjust her advice based on WHEN the question is asked:

BEFORE APRIL 2026: Protective award maximum = 90 days. 'One establishment' test applies (Woolworths). Unfair dismissal qualifying period = 2 years.

FROM APRIL 2026: Protective award DOUBLED to 180 days. Budget accordingly.

FROM JANUARY 2027: Unfair dismissal qualifying period = 6 months. Compensatory award UNCAPPED. 'One establishment' test replaced by WHOLE ORGANISATION counting. Every transfer with planned redundancies becomes higher risk.

Eileen must ALWAYS state the current position AND flag upcoming changes. Example: 'As at [today], the protective award maximum is [X]. From [date], this will increase to [Y]. You should plan on the basis of the higher figure if your redundancies will take effect after that date.'
sections.keyPrinciple: Intelligence is giving clarity when multiple instruments are active. An HR advisor who knows TUPE law but cannot guide a client through the practical process of a transfer with redundancies has failed. Eileen must be able to: identify every legal obligation that applies, sequence them into a practical workflow, flag the edge cases and risks, and adjust the advice based on when the question is asked. This is what separates Ailane from a legal database.
refs: ERA 1996 ss.166-170 — National Insurance Fund payments
ERA 1996 s.111A — protected conversations for voluntary departures
ERA 1996 s.13 — unlawful deduction from wages
ERA 2025 — protective award doubled, 'one establishment' reform, qualifying period reduction
commonErrors: Not advising employees properly on the consequences of OBJECTING to a transfer — objection terminates employment without dismissal, without redundancy pay, and without notice pay. Employees should be advised to seek legal advice before objecting

Not understanding the insolvency distinction — in a RESCUE insolvency (administration), employees transfer but certain debts are covered by the National Insurance Fund. In TERMINAL insolvency (liquidation), TUPE may not apply at all. The HR advisor must know which type of insolvency they are dealing with

Attempting downward harmonisation immediately after transfer — variations that are primarily because of the transfer are VOID. Wait, use other mechanisms (s.111A, restructure for genuine business reasons), or harmonise upward

Not conducting TUPE information and consultation AND collective redundancy consultation as SEPARATE processes — they have different triggers, different content, different representatives, and different remedies. Doing one does not satisfy the other

Not adjusting advice for the ERA 2025 timeline — the protective award doubles in April 2026, the qualifying period halves in January 2027, and the 'one establishment' test changes in 2027. HR advisors who give advice based on the current rules without flagging incoming changes are failing their clients

pendingAmendments.status: The 'one establishment' threshold for collective redundancy consultation will change to require employers to count redundancies across the WHOLE ORGANISATION. This fundamentally changes when collective consultation is triggered in transfer situations.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — will trigger collective consultation in many more situations

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — protective award doubled to 180 days (April 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: The maximum protective award for failure to collectively consult increases from 90 to 180 days' pay from April 2026.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — doubles the financial exposure for failure to consult

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — unfair dismissal qualifying period reduced to 6 months (January 2027)
pendingAmendments.status: Combined with the removal of the compensatory award cap, this increases the risk for transferees who dismiss transferred employees.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — more transferred employees will be able to bring unfair dismissal claims

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — ERA 2025 reforms will significantly affect collective redundancy thresholds from 2027
